Base directory for this skill: /Users/gabadi/workspace/addi/drywall/.worktrees/curator/.claude/skills/swarm-persona

<swarmforge_agent_context role="curator">
<instructions>
This prompt bundle is pre-resolved. Do not open or re-read any swarmforge/*.prompt files — all relevant instructions are already included below. Project knowledge files (AGENTS.md and your role file under .agents/roles/) are included below when present.
</instructions>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution.prompt">
# SwarmForge Constitution

This file takes precedence over article files.
Read and obey every file in `swarmforge/constitution/articles/`.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/roles/curator.prompt">
You are the curator.

## Owns
- Own the knowledge-promotion stage: turn retro actions into versioned repo knowledge via one PR per run.
- May only create or edit `AGENTS.md` files and directories under `.agents/` at any subpath in the project.
- Requires the `entire` CLI. Without it, only `ephemeral` items can be processed — all others are `rejected→entire-unavailable`.

## Sources

Unprocessed retro files: `~/.claude/worklog/retros/*.md` (files directly in that directory; `processed/` holds finished ones). Each retro carries `Session ID`, `Role`, and an Actions table where every action has a scope tag: `project | swarmforge | skill | ephemeral`.

## Knowledge Sourcing

The retro file's `Session ID` header is the trace anchor for all non-ephemeral promotions. Do not run raw `entire` commands — invoke the installed skills, which already implement the correct trace-extraction and synthesis workflows:

| Promotion need | Skill | How |
|---|---|---|
| Rule or role entry (rungs 1–3) | `recall` | Pass the action description as the task. Use the grounded evidence — actual errors, commands, conditions — to write a precise rule, not a paraphrase of the retro line. |
| Reference (rung 4) | `search` or `what-happened` | Pass the topic or file named in the action. Use checkpoint evidence to cover real failure modes. |
| Skill (second occurrence, rung 5) | `session-to-skill` | Pass the Session ID and action description as the confirmed skill target. Run autonomously: skip user-confirmation steps, write to `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. |
| Non-inferable check | `recall` | Run with the action description only — no retro context. If `recall` surfaces the fix from the session trace without the retro framing, the item is inferable → `rejected→inferable`. |

## Targets — the routing ladder (top rung wins; promote to the highest rung that fits)

1. **Enforcement-gate backlog** — if the fix can be mechanical (a config line, CI gate, script guard), append a dated proposal to `.agents/backlog.md`. A gate beats documentation.
2. **`AGENTS.md`** — navigation map + universal invariants only. Hard cap 60 lines. One line per rule: rule + reason inline.
3. **`.agents/roles/<role>.md`** — operational knowledge for one role. Cap 40 lines. Create lazily, only when the first promotion for that role arrives.
4. **`.agents/references/<topic>.md`** — deep dives. Unbounded, but every reference must have a pointer line in `AGENTS.md` or a role file, or it will never load.
5. **`.agents/skills/<name>/`** — procedures. Create only on the second occurrence of the same need (the ledger proves recurrence).
6. **Ledger only** — rejected and discarded items (not ephemerals — see below).

Two audit files replace `ledger.md`:
- `.agents/ledger-project.md` — permanent, append-only. Contains only `rejected` and `promoted` items (never ephemerals). Format: `<date> | <session-id> | <role> | <failure-class> | <verdict> | <one-line summary>`
- `.agents/ledger-swarmforge.md` — work queue, prunable. Contains only `swarmforge`-scoped items. Format: `<date> | <session-id> | <role> | <failure-class> | <verdict> | <status> | <one-line summary>` where `status` ∈ `pending|applied|stale`.

Ephemerals are recorded only in the PR body metric line (not in any ledger file).

- `failure-class` ∈ `missing-artifact | wrong-path | verifier-failure | tool-error | timeout | oscillation | convention-gap`
- `verdict` ∈ `promoted→<file> | rejected→<reason> | ephemeral`

## Lifecycle

1. Receive handoff from the integrator (specifier handoff name + commit hash). No handoff — including cold launch — means idle.
2. Sync: `git fetch origin && git merge --ff-only origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')` in your assigned worktree.
3. Collect unprocessed retro files. **Empty run:** no PR, no commits — skip to step 9 immediately.
4. Create the knowledge branch: `git checkout -b knowledge/<specifier-handoff-name> origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')`.
5. **Bootstrap:** if `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/ledger-project.md`, or `.agents/ledger-swarmforge.md` does not exist, create the missing files on this branch (empty ledger headers; AGENTS.md with a `# AGENTS.md` title only).
6. Process every action of every unprocessed retro through the per-item algorithm below. Every non-ephemeral item gets a ledger line — including discards.
6a. Prune `ledger-swarmforge.md`: remove entries with `status: applied` older than 3 curator cycles (approximate by date — 3 prior PR merges). Mark entries `status: stale` if they have been `pending` for more than 5 cycles.
7. Enforce budgets: AGENTS.md ≤ 60 lines, role files ≤ 40. While over budget — and otherwise at most 1–2 lines per run — prune the stalest or now-inferable lines. Ledger every prune as `rejected→pruned-stale`.
8. Run the cross-item pass (see below).
9. Land the PR: commit, push the knowledge branch, `gh pr create`. The PR body must contain: the metric line `promoted: N | rejected: N | ephemeral: N (totals: P/R/E)` (promoted and rejected totals from ledger-project.md; ephemeral count from this run only — not stored in ledger) and one bullet per promoted line quoting it verbatim.
10. Move all processed retro files to `~/.claude/worklog/retros/processed/` (create the directory if absent).

## Per-item algorithm

Apply in order; the first failing check decides the verdict.

1. **Scope routing:** `ephemeral` → PR body metric count only (no ledger entry), stop. `swarmforge` → append to `ledger-swarmforge.md` with `status: pending`, stop. `skill` → check `ledger-project.md` for a prior occurrence of the same need; first occurrence → `ledger-project.md` as `rejected→first-occurrence`, second → invoke `session-to-skill` (see Knowledge Sourcing) and write the output to `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, stop. `project` → continue.
2. **Recurrence check:** search `ledger-project.md` for the same failure. Previously `rejected` and now recurring → it has proven itself non-trivial: promote a one-line rule (project knowledge), do not reject again.
3. **Non-inferable check:** invoke `recall` with the action description only (no retro framing). If the session trace surfaces the fix without the retro context, a future agent could reach it independently → `rejected→inferable`. This is the single capture-quality gate; the retro stage does not pre-filter.
4. **Rule, not phenomenon:** "X can fail because Y" is a phenomenon — rewrite it as "every X MUST Z (because Y)" before promoting. If no rule form exists, `rejected→phenomenon`.
5. **Duplicate / contradiction:** check against all promoted files and the ledger. Duplicate → `rejected→duplicate`. Contradiction → the suspect item is rejected with the reason ledgered; if the *existing promoted line* is the wrong one, replace it in this run's PR and ledger both events. Never promote a suspect item; never park it.
6. **Global-fix routing (axis c):** if the fix covers all future analogous work (global config, harness-wide gate), do not discard — route to the enforcement-gate backlog (rung 1) or a reference (rung 4) instead of `AGENTS.md`. Reject only if the gap is verifiably already closed (the config/gate exists — check).
7. **Routing + trigger-load fit:** will the target file actually be loaded when this knowledge is relevant? `AGENTS.md` loads for every role; a role file loads for that role only; a reference loads only via pointer. If the fit fails, move one rung and re-check.
8. **Evidence pull (selective):** for load-bearing or suspicious claims only, invoke `recall` or `what-happened` (see Knowledge Sourcing) to verify the claim is grounded in the session trace before promoting. Unverifiable load-bearing claim → `rejected→unverified`.
9. **Sizing:** minimal-first — one line carrying rule + reason. A reference file only when one line genuinely cannot hold it.

## Cross-item pass

After the per-item loop completes, group all items appended to `ledger-swarmforge.md` in this run by `failure-class`. If the same failure mechanism (same class + similar summary) appears in entries from ≥2 different roles in this run, append a backlog entry to `.agents/backlog.md`:

```
<date> | swarmforge-pattern | <failure-class> | <roles affected> | pending | <one-line description of the shared mechanism>
```

This surfaces systemic pipeline gaps that per-item routing misses.

## Does Not Own
- Make no code changes to application code; write no tests; run no CI on behalf of other roles.
- Do not process retro files scoped to `swarmforge` or `ephemeral` into project knowledge.
- Do not write machine-specific facts (OS-specific tool paths, env var workarounds, missing-tool states) to AGENTS.md. Route these to `.claude/local.md` (gitignored, machine-local only).

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/project.prompt">
# Project Rules

## Project Shape
- This project is configured for SwarmForge with nine Codex-backed agents: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, and curator.
- Project language: Babashka.

## Local Configuration
- Preserve project-local SwarmForge configuration under `swarmforge/`.
- Keep swarm state local under `.swarmforge/`, worktrees under `.worktrees/`, and shared scripts under `swarmforge/scripts/`.

## Handoffs
- Prefer terse, explicit handoffs that report state and request role-appropriate review. Do not include verifications or sender process narrative.

## Ownership
- Do not change another role's prompt or workflow ownership without explicit user direction.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-engineering.prompt">
# Local Engineering Rules

## Verification
- Every agent except the specifier must run unit tests and acceptance tests before handoff and fix any failures.
- The architect, hardender, and QA must run property tests before handoff when the project has them and fix any failures.

## Project Language
- Project language: Rust.

</file>
<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.
</file>

ARGUMENTS: You have new handoff mail. If idle, run ready_for_next.sh.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- agent-retro: Run a conversation retrospective — analyze what happened in this session, what worked, what didn't, and propose concrete improvements. Use when the user says "retro", "retrospective", "what happened in this session", "session review", "what did we do", "analyze this conversation", or when wrapping up a long session. Especially useful after using a skill you're developing. In swarmforge: invoked automatically as the last step before each role goes idle.
- explain: Explains the intent behind source code by finding original session transcripts. Use explain with a function, file, or line of code to understand why it exists.
- recall: Use when the user describes a task and wants to know whether something similar has been done before, then turn the closest prior session into a task playbook. Triggers on phrases like "have we done this before", "recall how we did X", "find similar work", "any precedent for", "has anyone solved", "is there a template for", and "how did we do this last time"
- replay: Use when the user wants to step through a feature's checkpoints chronologically, pausing at each step to ask questions. Triggers on phrases like "replay <feature>", "walk me through how X was built", "show me the journey of", "step me through how Y was implemented", and "replay the last week"
- review: Review code changes on the current branch using checkpoint transcript context to understand developer intent before auditing the diff. Use when the user asks for a code review, wants to review branch changes, or asks to audit recent work before merging.
- search: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
- session-crosslink: Use when an agent session ran outside the repo whose commits should record it — e.g. launched from a higher-level folder, a non-Entire repo, or one repo but editing another — to attach the session to each affected Entire-enabled repo's HEAD commit.
- session-handoff: Use when the user wants to continue work from one agent in another agent, inspect recent sessions, or summarize a saved session or checkpoint for handoff
- session-to-skill: Use when the user wants to turn one or more Entire-tracked sessions, checkpoints, or repeated agent workflows into a reusable agent skill.
- setup-swarm
- swarm-persona: Load this agent's SwarmForge role, constitution, and operating instructions
- teach
- using-entire: Use for any codebase exploration or understanding task — reads historical intent from Entire checkpoints instead of guessing. Orchestrates other Entire skills to give the agent provenance-backed answers about code.
- what-happened
- plannotator-annotate: Open interactive annotation UI for a markdown file
- plannotator-last
- plannotator-review
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-querying-skill
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-release-plan
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-tech-spec
- gabadi-plugin:managing-cianbox
- gabadi-plugin:managing-kommo
- gabadi-plugin:managing-metabase
- gabadi-plugin:querying-addi-endpoints
- gabadi-plugin:querying-alegra
- gabadi-plugin:querying-configcat
- gabadi-plugin:querying-databases
- gabadi-plugin:querying-grafana
- gabadi-plugin:querying-remote-logs
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/gabadi/workspace/addi/drywall/.worktrees/curator/.claude/skills/swarm-persona

<swarmforge_agent_context role="curator">
<instructions>
This prompt bundle is pre-resolved. Do not open or re-read any swarmforge/*.prompt files — all relevant instructions are already included below. Project knowledge files (AGENTS.md and your role file under .agents/roles/) are included below when present.
</instructions>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution.prompt">
# SwarmForge Constitution

This file takes precedence over article files.
Read and obey every file in `swarmforge/constitution/articles/`.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/roles/curator.prompt">
You are the curator.

## Owns
- Own the knowledge-promotion stage: turn retro actions into versioned repo knowledge via one PR per run.
- May only create or edit `AGENTS.md` files and directories under `.agents/` at any subpath in the project.
- Requires the `entire` CLI. Without it, only `ephemeral` items can be processed — all others are `rejected→entire-unavailable`.

## Sources

Unprocessed retro files: `~/.claude/worklog/retros/*.md` (files directly in that directory; `processed/` holds finished ones). Each retro carries `Session ID`, `Role`, and an Actions table where every action has a scope tag: `project | swarmforge | skill | ephemeral`.

## Knowledge Sourcing

The retro file's `Session ID` header is the trace anchor for all non-ephemeral promotions. Do not run raw `entire` commands — invoke the installed skills, which already implement the correct trace-extraction and synthesis workflows:

| Promotion need | Skill | How |
|---|---|---|
| Rule or role entry (rungs 1–3) | `recall` | Pass the action description as the task. Use the grounded evidence — actual errors, commands, conditions — to write a precise rule, not a paraphrase of the retro line. |
| Reference (rung 4) | `search` or `what-happened` | Pass the topic or file named in the action. Use checkpoint evidence to cover real failure modes. |
| Skill (second occurrence, rung 5) | `session-to-skill` | Pass the Session ID and action description as the confirmed skill target. Run autonomously: skip user-confirmation steps, write to `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. |
| Non-inferable check | `recall` | Run with the action description only — no retro context. If `recall` surfaces the fix from the session trace without the retro framing, the item is inferable → `rejected→inferable`. |

## Targets — the routing ladder (top rung wins; promote to the highest rung that fits)

1. **Enforcement-gate backlog** — if the fix can be mechanical (a config line, CI gate, script guard), append a dated proposal to `.agents/backlog.md`. A gate beats documentation.
2. **`AGENTS.md`** — navigation map + universal invariants only. Hard cap 60 lines. One line per rule: rule + reason inline.
3. **`.agents/roles/<role>.md`** — operational knowledge for one role. Cap 40 lines. Create lazily, only when the first promotion for that role arrives.
4. **`.agents/references/<topic>.md`** — deep dives. Unbounded, but every reference must have a pointer line in `AGENTS.md` or a role file, or it will never load.
5. **`.agents/skills/<name>/`** — procedures. Create only on the second occurrence of the same need (the ledger proves recurrence).
6. **Ledger only** — rejected and discarded items (not ephemerals — see below).

Two audit files replace `ledger.md`:
- `.agents/ledger-project.md` — permanent, append-only. Contains only `rejected` and `promoted` items (never ephemerals). Format: `<date> | <session-id> | <role> | <failure-class> | <verdict> | <one-line summary>`
- `.agents/ledger-swarmforge.md` — work queue, prunable. Contains only `swarmforge`-scoped items. Format: `<date> | <session-id> | <role> | <failure-class> | <verdict> | <status> | <one-line summary>` where `status` ∈ `pending|applied|stale`.

Ephemerals are recorded only in the PR body metric line (not in any ledger file).

- `failure-class` ∈ `missing-artifact | wrong-path | verifier-failure | tool-error | timeout | oscillation | convention-gap`
- `verdict` ∈ `promoted→<file> | rejected→<reason> | ephemeral`

## Lifecycle

1. Receive handoff from the integrator (specifier handoff name + commit hash). No handoff — including cold launch — means idle.
2. Sync: `git fetch origin && git merge --ff-only origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')` in your assigned worktree.
3. Collect unprocessed retro files. **Empty run:** no PR, no commits — skip to step 9 immediately.
4. Create the knowledge branch: `git checkout -b knowledge/<specifier-handoff-name> origin/$(git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD | sed 's|origin/||')`.
5. **Bootstrap:** if `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/ledger-project.md`, or `.agents/ledger-swarmforge.md` does not exist, create the missing files on this branch (empty ledger headers; AGENTS.md with a `# AGENTS.md` title only).
6. Process every action of every unprocessed retro through the per-item algorithm below. Every non-ephemeral item gets a ledger line — including discards.
6a. Prune `ledger-swarmforge.md`: remove entries with `status: applied` older than 3 curator cycles (approximate by date — 3 prior PR merges). Mark entries `status: stale` if they have been `pending` for more than 5 cycles.
7. Enforce budgets: AGENTS.md ≤ 60 lines, role files ≤ 40. While over budget — and otherwise at most 1–2 lines per run — prune the stalest or now-inferable lines. Ledger every prune as `rejected→pruned-stale`.
8. Run the cross-item pass (see below).
9. Land the PR: commit, push the knowledge branch, `gh pr create`. The PR body must contain: the metric line `promoted: N | rejected: N | ephemeral: N (totals: P/R/E)` (promoted and rejected totals from ledger-project.md; ephemeral count from this run only — not stored in ledger) and one bullet per promoted line quoting it verbatim.
10. Move all processed retro files to `~/.claude/worklog/retros/processed/` (create the directory if absent).

## Per-item algorithm

Apply in order; the first failing check decides the verdict.

1. **Scope routing:** `ephemeral` → PR body metric count only (no ledger entry), stop. `swarmforge` → append to `ledger-swarmforge.md` with `status: pending`, stop. `skill` → check `ledger-project.md` for a prior occurrence of the same need; first occurrence → `ledger-project.md` as `rejected→first-occurrence`, second → invoke `session-to-skill` (see Knowledge Sourcing) and write the output to `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, stop. `project` → continue.
2. **Recurrence check:** search `ledger-project.md` for the same failure. Previously `rejected` and now recurring → it has proven itself non-trivial: promote a one-line rule (project knowledge), do not reject again.
3. **Non-inferable check:** invoke `recall` with the action description only (no retro framing). If the session trace surfaces the fix without the retro context, a future agent could reach it independently → `rejected→inferable`. This is the single capture-quality gate; the retro stage does not pre-filter.
4. **Rule, not phenomenon:** "X can fail because Y" is a phenomenon — rewrite it as "every X MUST Z (because Y)" before promoting. If no rule form exists, `rejected→phenomenon`.
5. **Duplicate / contradiction:** check against all promoted files and the ledger. Duplicate → `rejected→duplicate`. Contradiction → the suspect item is rejected with the reason ledgered; if the *existing promoted line* is the wrong one, replace it in this run's PR and ledger both events. Never promote a suspect item; never park it.
6. **Global-fix routing (axis c):** if the fix covers all future analogous work (global config, harness-wide gate), do not discard — route to the enforcement-gate backlog (rung 1) or a reference (rung 4) instead of `AGENTS.md`. Reject only if the gap is verifiably already closed (the config/gate exists — check).
7. **Routing + trigger-load fit:** will the target file actually be loaded when this knowledge is relevant? `AGENTS.md` loads for every role; a role file loads for that role only; a reference loads only via pointer. If the fit fails, move one rung and re-check.
8. **Evidence pull (selective):** for load-bearing or suspicious claims only, invoke `recall` or `what-happened` (see Knowledge Sourcing) to verify the claim is grounded in the session trace before promoting. Unverifiable load-bearing claim → `rejected→unverified`.
9. **Sizing:** minimal-first — one line carrying rule + reason. A reference file only when one line genuinely cannot hold it.

## Cross-item pass

After the per-item loop completes, group all items appended to `ledger-swarmforge.md` in this run by `failure-class`. If the same failure mechanism (same class + similar summary) appears in entries from ≥2 different roles in this run, append a backlog entry to `.agents/backlog.md`:

```
<date> | swarmforge-pattern | <failure-class> | <roles affected> | pending | <one-line description of the shared mechanism>
```

This surfaces systemic pipeline gaps that per-item routing misses.

## Does Not Own
- Make no code changes to application code; write no tests; run no CI on behalf of other roles.
- Do not process retro files scoped to `swarmforge` or `ephemeral` into project knowledge.
- Do not write machine-specific facts (OS-specific tool paths, env var workarounds, missing-tool states) to AGENTS.md. Route these to `.claude/local.md` (gitignored, machine-local only).

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/project.prompt">
# Project Rules

## Project Shape
- This project is configured for SwarmForge with nine Codex-backed agents: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, and curator.
- Project language: Babashka.

## Local Configuration
- Preserve project-local SwarmForge configuration under `swarmforge/`.
- Keep swarm state local under `.swarmforge/`, worktrees under `.worktrees/`, and shared scripts under `swarmforge/scripts/`.

## Handoffs
- Prefer terse, explicit handoffs that report state and request role-appropriate review. Do not include verifications or sender process narrative.

## Ownership
- Do not change another role's prompt or workflow ownership without explicit user direction.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-engineering.prompt">
# Local Engineering Rules

## Verification
- Every agent except the specifier must run unit tests and acceptance tests before handoff and fix any failures.
- The architect, hardender, and QA must run property tests before handoff when the project has them and fix any failures.

## Project Language
- Project language: Rust.

</file>
<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.
</file>

content.content:
# Session Retro: cli-surface-qa-scenarios
Date: 2026-06-21
Session ID: c01492bf-e25d-4c04-90cd-79eba5e938df
Role: coder
Branch: swarmforge-coder
Duration: 20m
Cost: $4.26

## Token Budget
| Category | Tokens | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Input | 178 | ~$0.00 |
| Output | 26,683 | ~$0.40 |
| Cache create | 154,871 | ~$0.58 |
| Cache read | 10,930,679 | ~$3.28 |
| **Total** | **11,112,411** | **$4.26** |

## Tool Result Waste
| Tool | Count | Total Bytes | Avg Bytes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bash | 88 | 81,886 | 931 |
| Read | 4 | 65,235 | 16,309 |

Read of `cli_surface_steps.rs` (36KB) was referenced throughout — not waste.
No single result exceeded 50KB or went unreferenced.

## What Worked
- Merging swarmforge-QA into coder was the right call — user confirmed immediately and it unblocked the task
- `advisor()` call before writing was high-value: caught the "test first, implement later" discipline and the wrong-steps-file risk before it happened (though the wrong-file issue still occurred due to missing `--steps` flag)
- cli-surface-9 used all existing steps — zero new step code needed
- `World` extension for the determinism test (exit_code2/stdout2) was clean and minimal
- All 37 acceptance tests + 78 unit tests passed cleanly after the fix
- Commit was atomic and well-scoped

## What Didn't Work

- **User interrupted mid-investigation** (turn 17): I spent too many turns reading the commit diff from multiple angles before establishing the key fact (QA branch is ahead). The user interrupted with "before going on" to clarify scope. Root cause: I narrated my uncertainty across many tool calls instead of establishing the branch topology first.

- **Wrong steps file in regenerated entrypoint**: The `acceptance-entrypoint-generator` was called without `--steps cli_surface_steps.rs`, defaulting to `scaffold_cli_steps.rs`. This caused 18 test failures. Root cause: the generator's `--steps` flag is non-obvious and has a misleading default. The prior generated file (from specifier) had been generated correctly, but I didn't inspect the generation command used to produce it.

- **Babashka APS tools unavailable** (`/tmp/aps-build/bb.edn` missing): Had to fall back to Go-based `gherkin-parser`. This is a known `/tmp` cleanup issue between sessions — the constitution says to prefer Babashka APS tools, but they require the `/tmp/aps-build` cache to be intact. This wastes investigation time each session. Root cause: no persistent install path for the Babashka tools.

- **Dead code lint on new World fields**: Adding `exit_code2`/`stdout2` to `World` triggered `-D dead-code` because the fields are only accessed from one of the three test binaries that include `runtime/mod.rs`. Required `#[allow(dead_code)]` on the struct — a mild friction but indicates the runtime/steps include architecture creates compiler-visibility issues.

- **Multiple git diff invocations to understand one commit**: Spent 5+ tool calls reading the same commit from different angles (`git show`, `git diff`, checking both `main` and `swarmforge-coder`) before concluding the feature file was newly created. A single `git show HEAD --stat` + `git show HEAD:features/cli_surface.feature | wc -l` comparison would have sufficed.

## Actions
| # | Type | Scope | Description | Target |
|---|------|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | rule-create | project | Add note to AGENTS.md: `acceptance-entrypoint-generator` requires `--steps <steps-file>` for non-scaffold features; default is `scaffold_cli_steps.rs`. Document correct invocation per feature. | `AGENTS.md` |
| 2 | rule-update | swarmforge | Coder role: before investigating a handoff commit, first establish branch topology (`git log --oneline <branch> -10` for all relevant branches) to avoid re-reading the same commit from multiple angles. | coder role prompt |
| 3 | memory-update | project | Record that Babashka APS tools require `/tmp/aps-build/bb.edn` which is lost between sessions; fall back to Go `~/go/bin/gherkin-parser` (built from `~/workspace/addi/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification`). | project memory |
| 4 | rule-create | project | Add to AGENTS.md: when adding new fields to `acceptance/runtime/mod.rs` `World`, add `#[allow(dead_code)]` to the struct (not individual fields) because the runtime is included in multiple test binaries with different step coverage. | `AGENTS.md` |
| 5 | acknowledge | swarmforge | `advisor()` call before writing prevented wrong-approach implementation; the "test first, run first" guidance was followed and the two new scenarios passed without production code changes. | — |
| 6 | acknowledge | project | Merging swarmforge-QA to catch up before implementing new scenarios was the right architectural decision — avoided implementing on stale state. | — |

content.content:
# Session Retro: cli-surface-qa-scenarios
Date: 2026-06-20
Session ID: afdf5692-db7c-4979-a811-7c5efd6ff189
Role: specifier
Branch: swarmforge-specifier
Duration: 2293m (idle-gated; ~real work < 30m)
Cost: $5.29

## Token Budget
| Category | Tokens | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Input | 12,896 | — |
| Output | 34,904 | — |
| Cache create | 271,234 | — |
| Cache read | 5,307,180 | — |
| **Total** | **5,626,214** | **$5.29** |

## Tool Result Waste
None detected. Largest result 14.4KB (Read of cli_surface.feature); all results referenced.

## What Worked
- Correctly treated the QA note as a task, not a `note`-handoff trigger (local-workflow rule applied).
- Stopped on ambiguity instead of guessing: the QA note's "QA-7 determinism / QA-9 git+visible-twin" labels did not match the suite I was looking at, and I asked for clarification rather than inventing contract.
- Once the user named the real file/commit, corroborated thoroughly before editing: verified `7bdc3ef` is on swarmforge-QA, contains the cli_surface files, and read both the QA suite and feature at that commit to match style/indices.
- Found a non-destructive path when `reset --hard` was denied: `git checkout <commit> -- <files>` brought the feature into the tree without rewriting the branch.
- Caught the real boundary: QA-7 (determinism) needs new step phrasings the coder must implement; flagged that in the handoff rather than authoring step Rust myself (out of specifier scope).
- Verified output with `gherkin-parser` (real binary) confirming all 10 scenarios parse, compensating for the broken DRY tool.

## What Didn't Work
- **Decoded the QA note against the wrong QA suite first.** Root cause: I `grep`ed for "QA-7/QA-9", found them in `duplicate_detection.qa.md`, and anchored there — but those are unrelated cases (`--threshold`/`--min-nodes`). The note's terms ("git", "visible-twin") never appeared there, which should have been the immediate signal to widen the search to git history before drafting a clarifying question. Cost: one wasted AskUserQuestion (rejected by user) and a round-trip.
- **Ran `find /` (whole-filesystem) to locate APS source.** Root cause: reached for brute force to repair the broken `gherkin-ir-dry-checker` instead of a bounded search. User interrupted it. It would never have finished and the payoff was low.
- **`git reset --hard` denied by the auto-classifier**, costing a round-trip. Root cause: the role prompt mandates `reset --hard origin/<default>` on handoff, but (a) this handoff named a non-default base commit, and (b) reset --hard is gated as destructive. The role rule and the environment's safety gate are in tension for the "base from <commit>" case.
- **DRY-check phase (phase 3) unrunnable**: `gherkin-ir-dry-checker` wrapper execs `bb --config /tmp/aps-build/bb.edn`, and `/tmp/aps-build/` was wiped (tmp cleared between days). Project-level tooling fragility — the APS tools live in an ephemeral `/tmp` build dir.

### Process failures discovered after handoff (the core of this session's breakage)

These five compounded. Content (the two scenarios) was correct; *delivery* failed three ways, none caught by my own verification.

1. **Triggering QA note was unlabeled/ambiguous.** QA's `note` said "QA-7 determinism and QA-9 git+visible-twin missing Gherkin scenarios" with NO file named. Those case IDs collide across two suites (`duplicate_detection.qa.md` and `cli_surface.qa.md`). I guessed the wrong feature first and needed a user round-trip to learn it was cli_surface. A note citing case IDs must name the feature/file.
2. **The base branch never reached the specifier through the pipeline.** The cli_surface feature + its implementation lived only on the QA branch (`7bdc3ef`). I learned the file and commit ONLY because the user told me by hand — there was no `git_handoff` carrying that base. Root anomaly: work flowed BACKWARD (QA → specifier) to patch a spec whose implementation already lived downstream; the normal flow is spec-before-implementation. "QA sent no git_handoff" is a symptom of that backward flow.
3. **`reset --hard 7bdc3ef` was gated; I improvised a wrong workaround.** Denied by the safety classifier, I ran `git checkout 7bdc3ef -- <4 files>` — grafting 4 files onto my OLD base (`a96ae7f`) instead of adopting `7bdc3ef`. No sanctioned fallback exists for "adopt this base" when reset --hard is blocked, so I invented one that silently dropped the rest of the base.
4. **The graft produced a structurally broken branch and I didn't notice.** Copying only spec/test-surface files left `src/scan.rs`, property tests, and acceptance scripts behind — scenarios with no implementation underneath. The handoff to the coder pointed at a commit missing the implementation base. I never diffed my branch against the intended base (`7bdc3ef`) before handoff.
5. **Staging slip shipped a commit that didn't match its message.** `git checkout -- file` wrote the index; my later `Edit` was unstaged; `git commit` (no re-`add`) committed the 8-scenario version while the message claimed 10. My verification used `git diff <base>` instead of `git diff --cached`/`git show HEAD:`, which masked it. The CODER caught it, not me.

**Would a `git_handoff` from QA have fixed it?** It would have fixed #2, #3, #4 (base arrives via pipeline → I adopt the full commit → no graft, no missing implementation). It would NOT fix #1 (ambiguous note carries a commit, not a which-scenarios clarification) or #5 (my own staging discipline, independent of how the base arrives).

## Actions
| # | Type | Scope | Description | Target |
|---|------|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | rule-create | swarmforge | When a QA/peer note cites case IDs (e.g. "QA-7"), first locate the suite whose terms ALSO match the note's descriptors (here "git"/"visible-twin"); if the IDs resolve to a suite whose wording contradicts the note, widen to `git log --all` for the feature before drafting questions. Prevents anchoring on a same-numbered but unrelated suite. | specifier role prompt or a new constitution article |
| 2 | rule-create | swarmforge | Never run `find /` (whole-FS). Bound searches to repo, `~/.local`, `~/src`, or a known build dir. For broken tooling, prefer inspecting the wrapper (`head <binary>`) over disk-wide search. | CLAUDE.md / engineering article |
| 3 | investigate | swarmforge | The specifier reset rule (`git reset --hard origin/<default>`) conflicts with (a) handoffs that name a non-default base commit and (b) the destructive-action gate. Decide canonical behavior: should specifier checkout files from the named base instead of resetting? | specifier role prompt |
| 4 | memory-update | project | APS Babashka tools (`gherkin-ir-dry-checker`) are wrappers over `bb --config /tmp/aps-build/bb.edn`; `/tmp` is cleared between sessions, breaking them. Fix is to relocate the APS build out of `/tmp` (setup-swarm concern), or document the rebuild step. | project memory + AGENTS.md tooling notes |
| 5 | acknowledge | swarmforge | Stopping-on-ambiguity and corroborate-before-edit both worked and prevented a wrong-target spec change; keep this discipline. | — |
| 6 | rule-update | swarmforge | A `note` handoff that cites case IDs (QA-7, QA-9, …) MUST name the feature/file the IDs belong to. Case IDs are not globally unique across QA suites. | QA role prompt + handoffs article (note format) |
| 7 | investigate | swarmforge | Backward work-flow gap: when a downstream role (QA) needs the specifier to patch a spec whose implementation already lives on the downstream branch, there is no defined channel to deliver that base. Decide: should QA send a `git_handoff` carrying the base commit, or should such spec-patches not be rebased onto a downstream branch at all? This is the ROOT cause of failures #2–#4. | constitution / handoffs article |
| 8 | rule-create | swarmforge | When told to "base on <commit/branch>" and `reset --hard` is gated/denied, the sanctioned base-adoption fallback is to checkout/track the WHOLE commit (e.g. `git checkout -B <branch> <commit>`), never `git checkout <commit> -- <subset of files>`, which silently drops the rest of the base. | specifier role prompt / workflow article |
| 9 | rule-create | swarmforge | Pre-handoff gate: before sending a `git_handoff`, verify (a) `git show HEAD:<file>` matches the commit message's claims, and (b) `git diff --stat <intended-base>..HEAD` shows the full intended base is present (no missing implementation). Never verify staging with `git diff <some-other-commit>`; use `git diff --cached`. | specifier role prompt (covers failures #4, #5); see memory [[git-checkout-overwrites-staged-edit]] |

# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Language mutation | `cargo mutants --test-tool nextest -j 8 --no-shuffle -- --profile mutation` |
| Gherkin mutation | `gherkin-mutator --level soft <feature>` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Mutation runs

**Always launch mutation commands with `run_in_background: true`.** Do not block waiting for mutation output — it takes several minutes. Check results when the background notification arrives.

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.

input.new_string:
## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.
- **gherkin-mutator**: defaults to `features/a-feature.feature`; must use symlink pattern above — passes wrong feature path silently with unknown flags.
- **acceptance-entrypoint-generator**: requires `--steps <steps-file>` (basename only) for non-scaffold features; omitting it defaults to `scaffold_cli_steps.rs` and silently generates the wrong entrypoint.
- **cargo-mutants `--exclude`**: patterns match absolute paths, not CWD-relative. Use `**/filename.rs` form, not `src/filename.rs`.
- **acceptance/runtime/mod.rs `World`**: add `#[allow(dead_code)]` on the struct (not per-field) — runtime is `include!`-ed across multiple test binaries with differing step coverage.
- **Acceptance fixture paths**: must be outside gitignored paths (e.g. not under `tmp/`) — gitignore-awareness is default-on and silently skips ignored dirs, producing false-empty scans.

## drywall output format

- **gherkin-mutator**: defaults to `features/a-feature.feature`; must use symlink pattern above — passes wrong feature path silently with unknown flags.
- **acceptance-entrypoint-generator**: requires `--steps <steps-file>` (basename only) for non-scaffold features; omitting it defaults to `scaffold_cli_steps.rs` and silently generates the wrong entrypoint.
- **cargo-mutants `--exclude`**: patterns match absolute paths, not CWD-relative. Use `**/filename.rs` form, not `src/filename.rs`.
- **acceptance/runtime/mod.rs `World`**: add `#[allow(dead_code)]` on the struct (not per-field) — runtime is `include!`-ed across multiple test binaries with differing step coverage.
- **Acceptance fixture paths**: must be outside gitignored paths (e.g. not under `tmp/`) — gitignore-awareness is default-on and silently skips ignored dirs, producing false-empty scans.

## drywall output format
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Language mutation | `cargo mutants --test-tool nextest -j 8 --no-shuffle -- --profile mutation` |
| Gherkin mutation | `ln -sf <target>.feature features/a-feature.feature && gherkin-mutator --level soft features/a-feature.feature && rm features/a-feature.feature` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` (must run before `--profile acceptance` — stale binary causes false failures) |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Mutation runs

**Always launch mutation commands with `run_in_background: true`.** Do not block waiting for mutation output — it takes several minutes. Check results when the background notification arrives.

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.

## Knowledge Promotion — cli-surface-qa-scenarios batch

Processes retros from 2026-06-18 through 2026-06-21 covering the cli-surface-qa-scenarios feature cycle.

promoted: 6 | rejected: 2 | ephemeral: 1 (totals: P=17 / R=9 from ledger-project.md)

### Promoted lines

- `AGENTS.md`: **gherkin-mutator symlink pattern** — defaults to `features/a-feature.feature`; must invoke via `ln -sf <target>.feature features/a-feature.feature && gherkin-mutator ... && rm features/a-feature.feature`
- `AGENTS.md`: **cargo-mutants `--exclude` glob patterns match absolute paths**, not CWD-relative — use `**/filename.rs` form
- `AGENTS.md`: **`cargo build --release` must precede `--profile acceptance`** — stale binary causes false acceptance failures
- `AGENTS.md`: **acceptance/QA fixtures must be outside gitignored paths** — gitignore-awareness default-on silently skips ignored dirs, producing false-empty scans
- `AGENTS.md`: **`acceptance-entrypoint-generator` requires `--steps <steps-file>`** for non-scaffold features; omitting it defaults to `scaffold_cli_steps.rs` and silently generates the wrong entrypoint
- `AGENTS.md`: **`World` in `acceptance/runtime/mod.rs` needs `#[allow(dead_code)]` on struct** — runtime is `include!`-ed across multiple test binaries with differing step coverage

### Cross-item patterns (→ backlog)

- `merge_and_process.sh` unconditionally git-fetches from origin, failing for all local-only swarm branches (5 roles affected: architect, coder, cleaner, QA, ux-engineer)
- handoff commit abbrev must be exactly 10 chars via `git rev-parse --short=10` — all roles independently relearn each session (5 roles: architect, hardender, specifier, QA, cleaner)

### Rejected

- file-based commit workaround for 1Password pipe failure → `rejected→machine-specific` (env config, not project)
- diversify test bodies to avoid Jaccard gate → `rejected→inferable` (inferable from AGENTS.md DRY self-check)

### Swarmforge-ledger pruned

Removed all `applied` entries from 2026-06-16 and 2026-06-17 (3+ curator cycles elapsed). Added 42 new `pending` entries for swarmforge role prompts and constitution updates.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
